credit_headline.narrative: Bank credit hit ₹212.1L Cr in April 2026, down ₹1.5L Cr from the March peak — the first month-on-month decline in this dataset. YoY growth slowed to 16.0% from 17.1%, still near the top of the range but off the March high. Non-food credit, which drives 99.5% of the system, fell ₹1.8L Cr, signaling post-year-end deleveraging or slower disbursements across agriculture, industry, services, and retail. All four sectors remain in positive YoY territory, so breadth is intact — but the momentum inflection is clear. Lenders should watch whether this is a one-period correction or the start of a sustained cooling cycle after FY26's record ₹31.2L Cr deployment.

sibc-bank-credit-abs.title: Bank credit dipped to ₹212.1L Cr — first decline in 7 periods
sibc-bank-credit-abs.observation: Total bank credit outstanding fell to ₹212.1L Cr in April 2026, down ₹1.5L Cr from the March 2026 peak of ₹213.6L Cr.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.direction: Volume declined ₹213.6L Cr → ₹212.1L Cr, breaking a six-period climb that had lifted credit from ₹178.7L Cr in January 2025.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.inference: The first month-on-month contraction since this dataset began signals post-year-end deleveraging or loan repayments. Watch whether this is a one-period correction or the start of a cooling cycle — the level remains in the upper range, but momentum has stalled.

sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.title: Bank credit grew 10% in FY25, 17% in FY26 — 7-point step-up
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.observation: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25 and 17.1% in FY26, a 7.1 percentage point acceleration.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.direction: This is the largest full-year step-up in the dataset, marking a sharp inflection from the prior year's single-digit growth.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.inference: The credit cycle has decisively turned. Lenders who scaled origination capacity in FY25 are now capturing outsized share; those who lagged face a widening competitive gap.

sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.title: Banks deployed ₹31.2L Cr in FY26 — highest annual add on record
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.observation: Banks deployed ₹31.2L Cr of net new credit in FY26, the highest annual addition in this window.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.direction: This figure has held constant across all seven periods in the dataset, as it reflects the full-year tally locked at March 2026 year-end.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.inference: The system added more credit in one year than in any prior period tracked here. Underwriting standards and portfolio concentration risk deserve close scrutiny at this scale.

sibc-bank-credit-yoy.title: Bank credit YoY slowed to 16.0% — down 1.1pp from March peak
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.observation: Bank credit grew 16.0% year-on-year in April 2026, down from 17.1% in March 2026.
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 17.1% → 16.0%, the first slowdown after a four-period acceleration from 10.0% in March 2025.
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.inference: The March year-end surge has given way to a modest cooling. The 16.0% rate still sits at the top of the historical range, but the trajectory has turned — watch for further deceleration if April's absolute decline extends into May.

sibc-food-credit-abs.title: Food credit surged to ₹1.04L Cr — highest level in dataset
sibc-food-credit-abs.observation: Food credit outstanding jumped to ₹1.04L Cr in April 2026, up ₹0.34L Cr from ₹0.70L Cr in March 2026.
sibc-food-credit-abs.direction: Volume spiked ₹0.70L Cr → ₹1.04L Cr, reversing the prior period's decline and reaching the highest level in this seven-period window.
sibc-food-credit-abs.inference: The sharp rebound likely reflects seasonal procurement financing for the new crop year. Food credit remains volatile and small (under 0.5% of total bank credit), but the swing is large enough to warrant monitoring for liquidity stress in agri-commodity chains.

sibc-food-credit-yoy.title: Food credit YoY slowed to 66.4% — down 26pp from March
sibc-food-credit-yoy.observation: Food credit grew 66.4% year-on-year in April 2026, down sharply from 92.4% in March 2026.
sibc-food-credit-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 92.4% → 66.4%, a 25.9 percentage point drop, moving from near the top of the range to the median.
sibc-food-credit-yoy.inference: The triple-digit growth rate is compressing as the base normalises. The April 2025 base was already elevated at ₹0.56L Cr, so the 66.4% rate reflects real volume growth, not just base effects — but expect further moderation as the year progresses.

sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.title: Non-food credit fell to ₹211.1L Cr — first drop after 6-period climb
sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.observation: Non-food credit outstanding declined to ₹211.1L Cr in April 2026, down ₹1.8L Cr from the March 2026 peak of ₹212.9L Cr.
sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.direction: Volume fell ₹212.9L Cr → ₹211.1L Cr, breaking a six-period climb from ₹178.1L Cr in January 2025.
sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.inference: Non-food credit — which accounts for 99.5% of total bank credit — is driving the system-wide contraction. The decline suggests post-year-end loan repayments or slower disbursements in agriculture, industry, services, and retail. Watch whether this is a one-month correction or the start of a sustained slowdown.

sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.title: Non-food credit added ₹30.8L Cr in FY26 — record annual deployment
sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.observation: Banks deployed ₹30.8L Cr of net new non-food credit in FY26, the highest annual addition in this window.
sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.direction: This figure has remained constant across all seven periods, as it reflects the full-year tally locked at March 2026 year-end.
sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.inference: Non-food credit accounts for 99% of the ₹31.2L Cr system-wide add. The growth is broad-based across productive sectors — a positive signal for economic activity, but watch for pockets of over-leverage.

sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.title: Non-food credit YoY eased to 15.8% — down 1.1pp from March
sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.observation: Non-food credit grew 15.8% year-on-year in April 2026, down from 16.9% in March 2026.
sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 16.9% → 15.8%, the first slowdown after a four-period acceleration from 9.9% in March 2025.
sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.inference: Non-food credit is cooling in lockstep with total bank credit. The 15.8% rate remains at the top of the historical range, but the trajectory has turned — lenders should prepare for tighter competition and margin pressure if the slowdown persists.

sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.title: All 4 main sectors grew YoY — breadth unchanged at maximum
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.observation: All four main credit sectors — agriculture, industry, services, retail — posted positive year-on-year growth in April 2026.
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.direction: Breadth has held at 4 out of 4 sectors for the entire seven-period window, with no sector slipping into contraction.
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.inference: Credit expansion remains broad-based, not concentrated. This reduces systemic risk but also means no sector is being starved — competition for quality assets is intense across the board.

psl.narrative: PSL growth is bifurcating sharply — Housing at 39.9%, Social Infrastructure at 35.3%, and Micro & Small at 28.5% are pulling away, while Export Credit and Others both contracted over 12%. The 52.4pp spread between top and bottom performers is the widest on record, up 3pp from the prior period. Banks are systematically reallocating PSL capital toward retail-facing, high-margin segments (housing, education, social infrastructure) and away from corporate/export categories. Micro & Small's transition to active status at 28.5% confirms MSME momentum is entrenched, not episodic. The strategic implication: PSL compliance is increasingly being met through retail and infrastructure lending, not traditional agriculture or export finance.

sibc-psl-agri-yoy.title: PSL Agriculture at 17.3% YoY — highest on record
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.observation: PSL Agriculture credit grew 17.33% year-on-year in Apr 2026, the strongest reading in the seven-period series.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.direction: Growth re-accelerated from 15.95% last period to 17.33%, extending a steady climb from 11.0% in Mar 2025 → 17.3% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.inference: Agriculture lending momentum is building through FY27. Banks are deploying more capital to this segment than at any point in this window — the trajectory suggests sustained strength as crop cycles and policy support align.

sibc-psl-education-yoy.title: PSL Education Loans hold at 23.3% YoY — structural shift intact
sibc-psl-education-yoy.observation: PSL Educational Loans grew 23.27% year-on-year in Apr 2026, holding near the upper end of the range.
sibc-psl-education-yoy.direction: Growth was near zero through Mar 2025 (0.9%), then surged to 23.5% in Jan 2026 and has held above 23% for four straight periods.
sibc-psl-education-yoy.inference: Education lending remains structurally re-rated. Banks are sustaining high deployment to student loans — this is not a one-off spike but a durable shift, likely driven by demand recovery and policy tailwinds.

sibc-psl-export-yoy.title: PSL Export Credit at -12.0% YoY — decline deepens again
sibc-psl-export-yoy.observation: PSL Export Credit contracted 12.02% year-on-year in Apr 2026, still negative and worsening from the prior period.
sibc-psl-export-yoy.direction: The contraction deepened from -8.36% last period to -12.02%, reversing the prior easing trend. Growth was positive at 5.3% in Mar 2025 → -17.2% in Jan 2026 → -12.0% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-export-yoy.inference: Export credit stabilisation has stalled. The renewed decline suggests trade finance demand remains weak or banks are pulling back — the trajectory is now uncertain, and a return to positive growth is no longer imminent.

sibc-psl-housing-yoy.title: PSL Housing at 39.9% YoY — highest on record, momentum intact
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.observation: PSL Housing credit grew 39.94% year-on-year in Apr 2026, the highest reading in this window.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.direction: Growth was negative through Mar 2025 (-1.1%), then surged to 37.9% in Jan 2026 and has accelerated further to 39.9% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.inference: Housing is the standout PSL segment. Banks are aggressively deploying capital to affordable housing — this is a structural re-rating, not a cyclical bounce. Expect continued strength as policy support and demand converge.

sibc-psl-medium-yoy.title: PSL Medium Enterprises at 17.2% YoY — lowest on record
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.observation: PSL Medium Enterprises credit grew 17.24% year-on-year in Apr 2026, the weakest reading in the seven-period series.
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.direction: Growth has decelerated steadily from 22.6% in Mar 2025 → 18.9% in Jan 2026 → 17.9% in Mar 2026 → 17.2% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.inference: Medium enterprises are losing momentum while Micro & Small are surging. Banks are shifting focus to smaller borrowers or seeing weaker demand from mid-sized firms — monitor for further deceleration or a floor near 17%.

sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.title: PSL MSME grew 13.4% in FY25, 29.4% in FY26 — 15.9pp step-up
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.observation: PSL MSME credit grew 13.4% in the year ending Mar 2025 and 29.4% in the year ending Mar 2026 — a 15.9-point acceleration, the largest in this dataset.
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.direction: Full-year growth more than doubled from FY25 to FY26, marking a sharp inflection in MSME lending.
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.inference: MSME is the fastest-accelerating PSL segment. Banks are pouring capital into small businesses — this is a structural shift, likely policy-driven and demand-led. Expect this to anchor PSL growth in FY27.

sibc-psl-msme-yoy.title: PSL Micro & Small at 28.5% YoY — upper range, slight deceleration
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.observation: PSL Micro & Small enterprises credit grew 28.53% year-on-year in Apr 2026, in the upper range of the series.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated slightly from 29.36% last period to 28.53%, after surging from 13.4% in Mar 2025 → 29.4% in Mar 2026.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.inference: Micro & Small remains the engine of PSL growth. The slight deceleration is noise — banks are sustaining aggressive deployment to the smallest borrowers. Watch for credit quality as volumes scale.

sibc-psl-others-yoy.title: PSL Others at -12.5% YoY — contraction deepens again
sibc-psl-others-yoy.observation: PSL Others contracted 12.45% year-on-year in Apr 2026, worsening from the prior period.
sibc-psl-others-yoy.direction: The contraction deepened from -9.55% last period to -12.45%, reversing the prior narrowing trend. The low was -23.9% in Jan 2026.
sibc-psl-others-yoy.inference: The 'Others' bucket has lost stabilisation momentum. The renewed decline suggests miscellaneous PSL categories are not finding footing — a return to positive growth is no longer imminent.

sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.title: PSL Renewable Energy positive for 8 straight periods — longest streak
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.observation: PSL Renewable Energy has posted positive year-on-year growth for 8 consecutive periods, the longest streak in this dataset.
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.direction: The streak extended from 7 periods last time to 8 now, with no interruption since the series began.
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.inference: Renewable Energy lending is entrenched in positive territory. Banks are consistently deploying capital to green projects — this is a structural trend, not a cyclical bounce. Expect sustained momentum.

sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.title: PSL Renewable Energy at 15.6% YoY — lowest on record, sharp deceleration
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.observation: PSL Renewable Energy grew 15.64% year-on-year in Apr 2026, the slowest reading in the seven-period series.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated sharply from 26.01% last period to 15.64%, and from a peak of 78.3% in Mar 2025 → 15.6% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.inference: Renewable Energy is still growing but has lost significant momentum. The sharp deceleration suggests normalisation after an exceptional FY25 — watch whether this stabilises above 15% or decelerates further toward single digits.

sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.title: PSL Social Infrastructure surged to 35.3% YoY — sharp rebound
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.observation: PSL Social Infrastructure grew 35.27% year-on-year in Apr 2026, rebounding sharply from 12.88% last period.
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.direction: Growth surged from 12.88% in Mar 2026 to 35.27% in Apr 2026, a 22.39-point jump. The series has been volatile: 61.9% in Mar 2025 → -1.0% in Feb 2026 → 35.3% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.inference: Social Infrastructure has returned to strong growth after erratic swings. This segment is highly volatile — the rebound suggests renewed deployment, but the trajectory warrants caution on sustainability given the prior instability.

sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.title: PSL Weaker Sections at 10.7% YoY — lowest on record
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.observation: PSL Weaker Sections (including PSLC SF/MF) grew 10.67% year-on-year in Apr 2026, the weakest reading in the seven-period series.
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated from 11.07% last period to 10.67%, and from a peak of 15.3% in Mar 2025 → 10.7% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.inference: Weaker Sections lending is losing momentum while Micro & Small and Housing are surging. Banks are prioritising higher-growth PSL categories — watch for further deceleration or policy interventions to revive this segment.

sibc-psl-yoy-scan.title: Housing 39.9%, Micro & Small 28.5% — PSL spread widens to 52.4pp
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.observation: Housing credit grew 39.9% YoY, Social Infrastructure 35.3%, and Micro and Small Enterprises 28.5% — the three fastest-growing PSL segments.
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.direction: The spread between top and bottom performers widened from 49.4pp to 52.4pp. Export Credit contracted 12.0% (vs -8.4% prior), Others declined 12.5% (vs -9.6% prior), while Weaker Sections grew 10.7% (vs 11.1% prior). Micro and Small Enterprises shifted from strengthening to active; Educational Loans moved from active to strengthening.
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.inference: PSL bifurcation is intensifying — the spread widened 3pp as Housing held near 40% and Export Credit's contraction deepened to double digits. Banks are doubling down on retail-facing PSL (housing, social infrastructure, education) while corporate/export categories continue to shrink. Micro & Small's shift to active status at 28.5% confirms sustained momentum in the MSME segment. The widening spread signals a structural reallocation: lenders are exiting low-margin, trade-linked PSL in favour of high-growth retail and infrastructure categories.

sector_mix.narrative: Services credit grew 18.6% in April 2026, down from a March peak of 19.0%, signalling the first deceleration after three periods of acceleration. Within services, growth is concentrating in Computer Software (52.7%, up 13.7 points) and NBFCs (27.7%, still strengthening), while four sub-sectors that were accelerating last period have now plateaued. Trade credit is decelerating across both Wholesale (18.1%, down from 20.1%) and Retail (8.0%, down from 11.6%), with Retail slowing faster despite gaining share. The overall services trajectory has shifted from broad-based acceleration to selective momentum in Software and NBFCs, with Trade and other sub-sectors losing steam. Lenders should tilt toward Software and NBFC exposure and watch for further deceleration in Trade and the broader services portfolio.

sibc-agriculture-share.title: Agriculture share steady at 12.4% — holding midpoint
sibc-agriculture-share.observation: Agriculture's share of total bank credit stands at 12.44% in Apr 2026.
sibc-agriculture-share.direction: Share edged up 0.02pp from 12.42% last period, holding near the median of its seven-period range (12.3%–12.6%).
sibc-agriculture-share.inference: Agriculture maintains a stable footprint in the credit mix — share stability despite decelerating growth signals the sector is expanding in line with the overall pool, neither gaining nor losing structural ground.

sibc-agriculture-yoy.title: Agriculture growth slows to 13.7% — down from 15.6% peak
sibc-agriculture-yoy.observation: Agriculture credit grew 13.70% YoY in Apr 2026, down from the prior period's peak of 15.62%.
sibc-agriculture-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 1.92pp from 15.62% → 13.70%, the first slowdown after a four-period climb (10.4% → 11.4% → 12.3% → 15.6%).
sibc-agriculture-yoy.inference: Agriculture lending momentum has moderated after peaking in Mar 2026 — the deceleration suggests the sharp upswing may be normalizing. Lenders should monitor whether this is a temporary pause or the start of a sustained slowdown, particularly in asset quality as the growth cycle matures.

sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.title: Industry credit 15.0% in FY26 vs 8.2% in FY25 — 6.8pp step-up
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.observation: Industry credit grew 8.2% in FY25 and 15.0% in FY26, marking a 6.8-point acceleration.
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.direction: This is the full-year acceleration measured at Mar 2026 year-end, reflecting a sharp step-up in annual growth momentum.
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.inference: Industry credit has re-rated decisively — the near-doubling of growth pace signals a structural shift, likely driven by capex revival, manufacturing push, or PLI-linked demand. This is the strongest industrial credit cycle in recent years.

sibc-industry-share.title: Industry share at 21.5% — lowest in seven periods
sibc-industry-share.observation: Industry accounts for 21.50% of total bank credit in Apr 2026, the lowest reading in this dataset.
sibc-industry-share.direction: Share slipped 0.03pp from 21.53% last period, holding at the bottom of its range (21.5%–21.9%).
sibc-industry-share.inference: Despite strong YoY growth, industry's share remains flat at the lower bound — it is growing in line with the overall credit pool, not gaining structural ground. The sector is recovering robustly but being outpaced by services and personal loans in the composition race.

sibc-industry-yoy.title: Industry credit at 15.1% YoY — highest on record
sibc-industry-yoy.observation: Industry credit grew 15.06% YoY in Apr 2026, the fastest rate in this seven-period window.
sibc-industry-yoy.direction: Growth held steady at 15.06% from 15.02% last period, sustaining the peak reached in Mar 2026 after a four-period climb: 8.2% → 12.1% → 13.5% → 15.0% → 15.1%.
sibc-industry-yoy.inference: Industry lending has plateaued at its highest level — the sustained 15%+ pace confirms a capex-driven recovery is entrenched. Lenders should prioritize sectors with strong order books and balance sheet repair, and watch for credit quality as the cycle matures.

sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.title: HFCs 17.9%, PFIs 16.9% — PFIs shift to active status
sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.observation: Within NBFC lending, Housing Finance Companies hold 17.9% share and Public Financial Institutions 16.9%.
sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.direction: PFIs shifted from strengthening to active status, narrowing the gap with HFCs. The spread between the two is 1.0pp, down from 1.2pp last period.
sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.inference: PFIs have moderated after a breakout phase but remain close to HFCs — the status shift to active suggests growth is stabilizing at elevated levels. HFCs retain the lead but PFIs are within striking distance for a share crossover if momentum resumes.

sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.title: PFIs surge 57.1% YoY, HFCs 16.7% — 40pp spread
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Public Financial Institutions grew 57.1% YoY, while Housing Finance Companies grew 16.7%. Both segments are strengthening.
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.direction: PFIs are growing at more than triple the pace of HFCs. The 40.4pp spread is the widest in this scan, up from 37.0pp last period.
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.inference: PFIs remain in a breakout phase — the explosive 57% growth likely reflects government-backed infra lending or policy-driven credit expansion. HFCs are growing steadily but PFIs are the clear outlier. Lenders must assess PFI asset quality and concentration risk given the extreme pace.

sibc-personal-loans-share.title: Personal loans at 33.0% share — back to upper range
sibc-personal-loans-share.observation: Personal loans account for 32.98% of total bank credit in Apr 2026, at the upper end of its range.
sibc-personal-loans-share.direction: Share rose 0.38pp from 32.59% last period (32.59% → 32.98%), rebounding from the prior period's low to match the historical high of 33.0%.
sibc-personal-loans-share.inference: Personal loans have regained ground in the credit mix after slipping in Mar 2026 — the sharp 0.38pp rebound signals renewed momentum. This reverses the prior narrative of losing share to productive sectors, suggesting retail credit demand remains robust and is reclaiming its structural footprint.

sibc-personal-loans-yoy.title: Personal loans 16.0% YoY — holding near peak
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.observation: Personal loans credit grew 16.01% YoY in Apr 2026, near the highest rate in this dataset.
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.direction: Growth held steady at 16.01% from 16.21% last period, sustaining the elevated pace reached in Mar 2026 after a four-period climb: 11.7% → 14.9% → 15.2% → 16.2% → 16.0%.
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.inference: Personal loans remain a growth engine at peak velocity — the sustained 16%+ pace confirms strong retail demand, likely driven by credit cards, personal loans, and vehicle finance. Lenders should monitor unsecured exposure and delinquency trends as growth plateaus at elevated levels.

sibc-services-fy-acceleration.title: Services credit 19.0% in FY26 vs 12.0% in FY25 — 7.0pp jump
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.observation: Services credit grew 12.0% in FY25 and 19.0% in FY26, marking a 7.0-point acceleration.
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.direction: This is the full-year acceleration measured at Mar 2026 year-end, reflecting the sharpest annual step-up in this dataset.
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.inference: Services credit has re-accelerated decisively — the 7-point jump signals broad-based demand across trade, NBFCs, and other services. This is the strongest services credit cycle in recent years, driven by consumption recovery and NBFC expansion.

sibc-services-share.title: Services share at 28.2% — down from Mar peak
sibc-services-share.observation: Services account for 28.21% of total bank credit in Apr 2026.
sibc-services-share.direction: Share declined 0.26pp from 28.47% last period (28.47% → 28.21%), slipping from the prior period's peak but holding near the upper quartile of its range (27.8%–28.5%).
sibc-services-share.inference: Services have given back some ground after reaching a structural high in Mar 2026 — the 0.26pp decline suggests other sectors (personal loans, agriculture) are growing faster this period. Services remain elevated but the share trajectory has reversed, signaling a pause in the sector's structural gain.

sibc-services-share-scan.title: NBFCs 34.5%, Trade 22.1% — NBFCs shift to active, Trade weakens
sibc-services-share-scan.observation: Within services credit, NBFCs hold 34.5% share, Trade 22.1%, and Other Services 20.5%. Shipping, Computer Software, and Aviation are the smallest at 0.2%, 0.9%, and 0.9% respectively.
sibc-services-share-scan.direction: NBFCs shifted from strengthening to active status, while Trade shifted from active to weakening. The spread between the top (NBFCs) and bottom (Shipping) is 34.4pp, up from 34.0pp last period.
sibc-services-share-scan.inference: NBFCs remain the dominant force in services credit but momentum has moderated — the shift to active suggests growth is stabilizing at elevated levels. Trade is now weakening, signaling a slowdown in trade finance demand. Lenders should watch for a rotation within services: NBFCs consolidating gains while trade-linked credit cools.

sibc-services-yoy.title: Services credit at 18.6% YoY — momentum plateaus after March peak
sibc-services-yoy.observation: Services credit grew 18.6% year-on-year in April 2026, down 0.4 points from the prior period.
sibc-services-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated from 19.0% in March 2026 to 18.6% in April 2026. The trajectory over the past year shows: 12.0% → 15.5% → 16.3% → 19.0% → 18.6%. The March peak was the highest in this window; April marks the first pullback after three periods of acceleration.
sibc-services-yoy.inference: Services credit remains in the upper range of its historical distribution but has lost momentum after peaking in March. The 0.4-point decline is modest, but it breaks a three-period acceleration streak. Lenders should watch the next period closely — if growth continues to decelerate, the March peak may have been a cyclical high rather than a new structural level. Services is still the fastest-growing major sector, but the trajectory has shifted from accelerating to plateauing.

sibc-services-yoy-scan.title: Computer Software surges to 52.7% — 6 of 10 services sub-sectors active
sibc-services-yoy-scan.observation: All 10 services sub-sectors are growing year-on-year. Computer Software leads at 52.7%, followed by Shipping at 40.7% and NBFCs at 27.7%. Transport Operators trails at 8.7%.
sibc-services-yoy-scan.direction: The spread between top and bottom widened to 44.1 percentage points, up from 32.5 points last period. Computer Software shifted from active to strengthening status and jumped from 39.0% to 52.7% — a 13.7-point acceleration. Shipping, Tourism, Commercial Real Estate, and Trade all shifted from strengthening back to active, indicating their growth rates have stabilised after prior acceleration.
sibc-services-yoy-scan.inference: Services credit growth is now concentrating in Computer Software, which is pulling away from the pack with a 13.7-point surge. The widening spread and status shifts signal a narrowing of momentum — four sub-sectors that were accelerating last period have now plateaued. Shipping remains strong at 40.7% but has stopped accelerating. NBFCs at 27.7% continue to strengthen. Lenders should prioritise Software and NBFC exposure — these are the only two sub-sectors still accelerating. The broader services portfolio is growing but no longer broadening.

sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.title: Retail Trade hits 44.4% share — reverses Wholesale dominance trend
sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Trade credit, Wholesale holds 55.6% share and Retail 44.4%, an 11.1 percentage point spread. Retail is strengthening while Wholesale is weakening.
sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.direction: The spread narrowed from 12.7 points last period to 11.1 points. Status shifts reversed: Wholesale moved from strengthening to weakening, while Retail moved from weakening to strengthening. Retail is now gaining share after losing ground in the prior period.
sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.inference: Retail Trade is clawing back share from Wholesale, reversing the prior period's divergence. The 1.6-point narrowing in one period signals Retail is growing faster than Wholesale in absolute terms, even if its YoY rate is lower (see YoY scan below). This shift suggests consumer-facing commerce is regaining momentum, possibly driven by festive or seasonal demand. Lenders should rebalance Trade exposure — Retail is no longer the laggard.

sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.title: Wholesale Trade at 18.1% YoY — Retail lags at 8.0%
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Wholesale Trade credit grew 18.1% year-on-year and Retail Trade grew 8.0%, both active. The growth spread between them is 10.1 percentage points.
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Both sub-sectors shifted from strengthening to active status, indicating their growth rates have stabilised after prior acceleration. The spread widened from 8.5 points last period to 10.1 points. Wholesale decelerated from 20.1% to 18.1% (down 2.0 points), while Retail decelerated from 11.6% to 8.0% (down 3.6 points).
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Trade credit growth is decelerating across both Wholesale and Retail, but Retail is slowing faster — it lost 3.6 points versus Wholesale's 2.0-point decline. The widening growth spread means Wholesale is still pulling away in percentage terms, even as both sub-sectors plateau. Retail's 8.0% growth is the weakest reading for either sub-sector in this window. Despite Retail gaining share (see share scan above), its YoY momentum is fading. Lenders should monitor Retail Trade closely — if growth continues to decelerate, the share gain may be temporary, driven by Wholesale slowing even faster rather than Retail strengthening.

retail.narrative: Personal loans are in a broad-based expansion, with six of nine categories growing and three holding near recent peaks. Gold loans remain the structural outlier at 121% YoY — the sixth consecutive triple-digit period — but the explosive phase is moderating (down from 128.8% in Jan 2026). Vehicles have re-accelerated sharply to 18.0% YoY after a weak FY25, and other PL has nearly doubled its growth rate to 12.7%. Housing is growing at a healthy 11.4%, but it is losing share (now 48.2%, down from 50.4% a year ago) because gold, vehicles, and other PL are capturing incremental credit faster. The mix is shifting: housing remains the anchor at 48% share, but gold and vehicles are the momentum plays. Consumer durables and advances against shares are contracting, but both are immaterial at <0.5% share combined. Lenders focused on housing alone are missing the fastest-growing pockets — gold, vehicles, and other PL are where incremental credit is flowing.

sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.title: Credit cards below 10% YoY for 4 straight periods — longest run
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.observation: Credit card growth has remained below 10% YoY for 4 consecutive periods, the longest such run in this dataset.
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.direction: The sub-10% streak extended from 3 periods in Mar 2026 to 4 periods in Apr 2026.
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.inference: Credit card lending remains structurally subdued. The streak extension confirms this is not a temporary pause but a sustained regime shift — tighter underwriting and regulatory scrutiny have permanently reset card origination volumes.

sibc-pl-cc-share.title: Credit card share at 4.28% — marginal uptick from record low
sibc-pl-cc-share.observation: Credit cards now account for 4.28% of personal loans, up 0.04 points from the prior period's record low of 4.24%.
sibc-pl-cc-share.direction: Share trajectory: 5.0% → 4.9% → 4.8% → 4.4% → 4.3% → 4.2% → 4.3%. The latest reading is a minor uptick but remains near the bottom of the range.
sibc-pl-cc-share.inference: The marginal uptick is noise, not a reversal. Cards continue to lose ground within personal loans as gold and housing capture incremental flows — this structural shift is intact.

sibc-pl-cc-yoy.title: Credit card growth at 3.78% YoY — stabilising in low single digits
sibc-pl-cc-yoy.observation: Credit card outstanding grew 3.78% YoY in Apr 2026, up from 3.55% in Mar 2026.
sibc-pl-cc-yoy.direction: Growth trajectory: 13.0% → 11.2% → 10.6% → 1.5% → 1.7% → 3.5% → 3.8%. The latest reading is a 0.2-point uptick, holding near the median of the recent range.
sibc-pl-cc-yoy.inference: Growth has stabilised in the 3–4% band after the sharp deceleration from 13% a year ago. This is the new normal for card lending — lenders should focus on yield optimisation and portfolio quality, not volume expansion.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.title: Consumer Durables negative for 6 straight periods — record contraction
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.observation: Consumer Durables have posted negative YoY growth for 6 consecutive periods, the longest contraction streak on record.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.direction: The negative streak extended from 5 periods in Mar 2026 to 6 periods in Apr 2026.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.inference: Consumer durable financing is in structural decline. The record-length contraction confirms lenders have exited this segment — no meaningful origination or portfolio recovery is expected unless consumption rebounds sharply.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.title: Consumer Durables share flat at 0.32% — shrinking into irrelevance
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.observation: Consumer Durables represent 0.32% of personal loans, unchanged from the prior period and near the bottom of the range.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.direction: Share trajectory: 0.4% → 0.4% → 0.4% → 0.3% → 0.3% → 0.3% → 0.3%. The segment has lost 0.1 points since Jan 2025 and remains at the floor.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.inference: This segment is shrinking into irrelevance within retail credit. Lenders have effectively exited — no strategic priority or portfolio growth expected.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.title: Consumer Durables -3.38% YoY — decline moderating from -5.34%
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.observation: Consumer Durables outstanding contracted 3.38% YoY in Apr 2026, an improvement from the -5.34% decline in Mar 2026.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.direction: Growth trajectory: -2.4% → 2.6% → -1.0% → -4.0% → -9.8% → -5.3% → -3.4%. The decline has moderated by 2.0 points from the prior period.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.inference: The worst of the contraction may be behind us, but the segment remains in decline. Recovery is not yet visible — lenders should continue to de-prioritise this category.

sibc-pl-education-share.title: Education loans at 2.24% share — lowest in this window
sibc-pl-education-share.observation: Education loans account for 2.24% of personal loans, down 0.01 points from the prior period and the smallest share in 7 periods.
sibc-pl-education-share.direction: Share trajectory: 2.3% → 2.3% → 2.3% → 2.3% → 2.3% → 2.2% → 2.2%. The segment has drifted down 0.1 points since Jan 2025.
sibc-pl-education-share.inference: Education lending is growing but losing ground relative to faster-moving segments like gold and housing. This is a stable, low-volatility portfolio — not a growth engine.

sibc-pl-education-yoy.title: Education loans at 13.27% YoY — decelerating to lowest rate
sibc-pl-education-yoy.observation: Education loans grew 13.27% YoY in Apr 2026, down from 13.36% in Mar 2026 and the slowest rate in 7 periods.
sibc-pl-education-yoy.direction: Growth trajectory: 15.9% → 15.3% → 15.1% → 14.0% → 13.7% → 13.4% → 13.3%. The rate has declined 2.6 points over 15 months.
sibc-pl-education-yoy.inference: Education lending is decelerating gradually but remains in double digits. This is a defensive, low-risk segment — expect continued mid-teens growth but no acceleration.

sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.title: FD advances share at 2.27% — sharp 0.17pp drop from prior peak
sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.observation: Advances against Fixed Deposits now represent 2.27% of personal loans, down 0.17 points from the prior period's 2.44%.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.direction: Share trajectory: 2.3% → 2.3% → 2.4% → 2.3% → 2.3% → 2.4% → 2.3%. The latest reading is a sharp reversal from the prior period's peak.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.inference: The prior period's share peak was likely driven by a temporary surge; the drop back to 2.3% suggests normalisation. FD-backed lending remains a defensive play but is not gaining structural ground.

sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.title: FD advances at 9.61% YoY — sharp 9.6pp drop from 19.22%
sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.observation: Advances against Fixed Deposits grew 9.61% YoY in Apr 2026, down sharply from 19.22% in Mar 2026 and the slowest rate in 7 periods.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.direction: Growth trajectory: 17.1% → 12.8% → 13.4% → 13.1% → 14.1% → 19.2% → 9.6%. The latest reading is a 9.6-point drop from the prior period's peak.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.inference: The prior period's 19.2% spike was an outlier, likely driven by a temporary surge. The drop to 9.6% is a sharp reversal — FD-backed lending is decelerating, not accelerating. The structural pivot toward collateralised credit is less pronounced than the prior period suggested.

sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.title: Gold loans positive for 8 straight periods — longest expansion ever
sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.observation: Gold loans have posted positive YoY growth for 8 consecutive periods, the longest unbroken expansion streak in this dataset.
sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.direction: The positive streak extended from 7 periods in Mar 2026 to 8 periods in Apr 2026.
sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.inference: Gold lending is the most consistent growth story in retail credit. The record-length streak confirms this momentum is entrenched — driven by high gold prices, strong rural demand, and lender appetite for secured lending.

sibc-pl-gold-share.title: Gold loans at 7.02% share — highest ever, up 0.37pp
sibc-pl-gold-share.observation: Gold loans now account for 7.02% of personal loans, up 0.37 points from the prior period and the highest share in this dataset.
sibc-pl-gold-share.direction: Share trajectory: 3.0% → 3.2% → 3.5% → 6.0% → 6.3% → 6.6% → 7.0%. The segment has more than doubled its share in 15 months.
sibc-pl-gold-share.inference: Gold lending has become a structural pillar of retail credit, displacing unsecured products. This is the single biggest portfolio shift in personal loans — lenders should expand gold loan capacity aggressively to capture this sustained demand.

sibc-pl-gold-yoy.title: Gold loans at 121.2% YoY — sixth straight triple-digit period
sibc-pl-gold-yoy.observation: Gold loans grew 121.2% YoY in Apr 2026, down from 123.7% in Mar 2026.
sibc-pl-gold-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated by 2.5 points period-on-period (123.7% → 121.2%), continuing the gradual cooling from the Feb 2026 peak of 127.9%.
sibc-pl-gold-yoy.inference: Triple-digit growth has now persisted for six consecutive periods, confirming sustained structural demand. The deceleration from 128.8% in Jan 2026 to 121.2% suggests the explosive phase is moderating, but gold remains the fastest-growing personal loan category by a factor of 6×.

sibc-pl-housing-share.title: Housing share falls to 48.2% — lowest on record
sibc-pl-housing-share.observation: Housing loans now account for 48.2% of personal loans, down from 48.4% in Mar 2026.
sibc-pl-housing-share.direction: Share has declined steadily from 50.4% in Jan 2025 to 48.2% in Apr 2026, losing 2.2 points over five quarters.
sibc-pl-housing-share.inference: Housing is losing ground in a growing personal loan pool — gold, vehicles, and other PL are capturing incremental credit faster. This is a structural shift: housing growth is healthy at 11.4%, but it is being outpaced by categories growing at 18–121%.

sibc-pl-housing-yoy.title: Housing loans at 11.4% YoY — holding near upper range
sibc-pl-housing-yoy.observation: Housing loans grew 11.4% YoY in Apr 2026, down marginally from 11.5% in Mar 2026.
sibc-pl-housing-yoy.direction: Growth has stabilized in the 11.0%–11.5% range since Jan 2026, after running at 10.7%–11.1% in early 2025.
sibc-pl-housing-yoy.inference: Housing growth has re-accelerated and is now holding near the top of its recent range. Demand is healthy, but the 11.4% pace is still being outrun by gold (121%) and vehicles (18%) — explaining the continued share loss. Housing remains the anchor, but not the momentum play.

sibc-pl-other-share.title: Other PL share at 24.9% — lowest in seven periods
sibc-pl-other-share.observation: Other Personal Loans account for 24.9% of personal loans, down from 25.0% in Mar 2026.
sibc-pl-other-share.direction: Share has declined from 25.8% in Jan 2025 to 24.9% in Apr 2026, losing 0.9 points over five quarters.
sibc-pl-other-share.inference: Despite accelerating growth (12.7% YoY), Other PL is losing share — a sign that gold and vehicles are growing even faster. This category is still the second-largest by share, but it is no longer the incremental growth leader.

sibc-pl-other-yoy.title: Other PL at 12.7% YoY — growth holding near peak
sibc-pl-other-yoy.observation: Other Personal Loans grew 12.7% YoY in Apr 2026, down marginally from 12.8% in Mar 2026.
sibc-pl-other-yoy.direction: Growth has accelerated sharply from 7.5% in Mar 2025 to 12.7% in Apr 2026, and has held in the 12.1%–12.8% range for the last three periods.
sibc-pl-other-yoy.inference: A clear inflection: growth has nearly doubled year-on-year and is now holding near its peak. This category — which includes personal loans not classified elsewhere — has regained momentum, likely driven by consumption and working capital needs. The trajectory has stabilized after the sharp re-acceleration.

sibc-pl-share-advances-share.title: Advances against shares at 0.1% — immaterial to mix
sibc-pl-share-advances-share.observation: Advances against shares and bonds account for 0.1% of personal loans, down from 0.2% in Mar 2026.
sibc-pl-share-advances-share.direction: Share has fluctuated between 0.1% and 0.2% across seven periods, with no sustained trend.
sibc-pl-share-advances-share.inference: This is a niche product with no material impact on personal loan composition. At 0.1% share, movements in this category are irrelevant to portfolio strategy.

sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.title: Advances against shares turn negative at -3.6% YoY
sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.observation: Advances against shares contracted 3.6% YoY in Apr 2026, down from +4.6% growth in Mar 2026.
sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.direction: Growth collapsed from 33.0% in Jan 2025 to -3.6% in Apr 2026, turning negative for the first time in this window after a brief stabilization at 2.8%–4.6% in Feb–Mar 2026.
sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.inference: The sharp deceleration from a high base has now turned into outright contraction, likely reflecting tighter underwriting or reduced demand for leveraged equity exposure. At 0.1% share, this category is immaterial to overall personal loan dynamics.

sibc-pl-share-scan.title: Housing at 48.2%, Other PL 24.9%, Vehicles 10.7% — top 3 hold 84%
sibc-pl-share-scan.observation: Housing accounts for 48.2% of personal loans, followed by Other PL at 24.9% and Vehicles at 10.7%.
sibc-pl-share-scan.direction: The top three categories hold 84% of personal loans. Housing's lead has narrowed from 50.4% in Jan 2025 to 48.2% in Apr 2026, while gold (6.6% share, not in top 3) and vehicles capture incremental flows.
sibc-pl-share-scan.inference: The personal loan mix is shifting: housing remains the anchor, but gold (121% YoY) and vehicles (18% YoY) are the momentum plays. Lenders focused on housing alone are missing the fastest-growing pockets. At the bottom, advances against shares (0.1%) and consumer durables (0.3%) are immaterial.

sibc-pl-vehicle-share.title: Vehicle loans at 10.7% share — holding steady
sibc-pl-vehicle-share.observation: Vehicle loans account for 10.7% of personal loans, up marginally from 10.6% in Mar 2026.
sibc-pl-vehicle-share.direction: Share has held in the 10.4%–10.7% range across seven periods, with no meaningful drift.
sibc-pl-vehicle-share.inference: Vehicles are growing fast (18.0% YoY) but in line with the overall personal loan pool — they are not gaining share but are a reliable growth contributor. The stable share confirms that vehicle lending is keeping pace with the broader personal loan expansion.

sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.title: Vehicle loans at 18.0% YoY — second-fastest major category
sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.observation: Vehicle loans grew 18.0% YoY in Apr 2026, down from 18.6% in Mar 2026.
sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.direction: Growth more than doubled from 8.6% in Mar 2025 to 18.0% in Apr 2026, and has held in the 17.1%–18.6% range since Jan 2026.
sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.inference: A sharp inflection: vehicle lending has re-accelerated after a weak FY25 and is now holding near its peak. This likely reflects pent-up demand, improved affordability, or aggressive dealer financing. Vehicles are now the second-fastest growing major personal loan category after gold, and the trajectory has stabilized at elevated levels.

sibc-pl-yoy-scan.title: Gold at 121%, Vehicles 18%, Education 13% — 6 of 9 active
sibc-pl-yoy-scan.observation: Gold loans grew 121.2% YoY, followed by vehicles at 18.0% and education at 13.3%. Consumer durables contracted 3.4%, and advances against shares fell 3.6%.
sibc-pl-yoy-scan.direction: Six of nine categories are active, two are declining (consumer durables, advances against shares), and one is strengthening (credit cards at 3.8%). The spread between the fastest and slowest is 124.8 points.
sibc-pl-yoy-scan.inference: Broad-based momentum across personal loans, with gold still the outlier. Housing (11.4%), vehicles (18.0%), and other PL (12.7%) are all active and holding near recent peaks — a sign that the personal loan cycle is gaining momentum beyond gold. Consumer durables and advances against shares are the lone weak spots, but both are immaterial at <0.5% share combined. Credit cards are strengthening from a low base.

industry.narrative: Industry credit is in the midst of a historic reallocation: Micro & Small enterprises are growing at 30.1% YoY for the fourth straight period, capturing 3pp of share from large corporates over the past year. Large corporates hit their fastest growth on record at 10.0%, but it is not enough to defend their dominance — their share has fallen to 67.2%, the lowest in this dataset. The MSME vs large corporate growth spread has narrowed from 25.7pp to 20.1pp as large corporates re-accelerate, but MSME still commands the incremental credit opportunity. Within Textiles, Jute is surging at 19.9% while the two dominant segments (Other and Cotton) grow at more modest mid-single to mid-teens rates. The strategic message: lenders with MSME exposure are riding the fastest-growing segments in industry credit, while those focused on large corporates are playing catch-up despite their own acceleration.

sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.title: Iron & Steel share slips to 68.2% — Other Metal gains ground
sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.observation: Iron and Steel holds 68.2% of Basic Metal credit, while Other Metal and Metal Products accounts for 31.8%.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.direction: Iron and Steel's share fell from 68.8% to 68.2%, shifting from strengthening to weakening. Other Metal and Metal Products rose from 31.2% to 31.8%, moving from weakening to strengthening. The spread narrowed from 37.7 to 36.4 percentage points.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.inference: Iron and Steel's dominance is eroding for the first time in this window. Other Metal Products is clawing back share despite the smaller base — lenders are diversifying within Basic Metal, likely backing aluminium, copper, and specialty alloys as steel demand moderates.

sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.title: Other Metal Products leads at 19.9% YoY — Iron & Steel at 16.7%
sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Other Metal and Metal Products grew 19.9% year-on-year, while Iron and Steel expanded 16.7%.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Other Metal Products' growth slowed from 21.0% to 19.9%. Iron and Steel accelerated from 18.8% to 16.7%, shifting from strengthening to active. The spread narrowed from 2.2 to 3.3 percentage points.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Other Metal Products maintains its growth lead, confirming sustained demand for non-steel metals. Iron and Steel's deceleration to 16.7% signals cooling momentum — watch for demand-side headwinds in construction and automotive steel consumption.

sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.title: Pharma share slips to 33.5% — Fertiliser stabilises at 10.2%
sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Chemicals, Others holds 44.1%, Drugs and Pharmaceuticals 33.5%, Petro Chemicals 12.2%, and Fertiliser 10.2%.
sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.direction: Drugs and Pharmaceuticals' share fell from 33.9% to 33.5%, shifting from strengthening to active. Fertiliser rose from 9.9% to 10.2%, moving from weakening to active. The spread narrowed from 34.3 to 33.8 percentage points.
sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.inference: Pharma's share loss marks a reversal after prior gains — growth is slowing relative to the broader Chemicals pool. Fertiliser's stabilisation at 10.2% suggests the worst of the contraction may be over, but it remains the smallest segment.

sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.title: Pharma surges to 20.6% YoY — Fertiliser flat at -0.3%
sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Drugs and Pharmaceuticals grew 20.6% year-on-year, Petro Chemicals 19.4%, and Others 16.4%. Fertiliser contracted -0.3%.
sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Pharma accelerated from 17.9% to 20.6%, shifting to strengthening. Petro Chemicals decelerated sharply from 27.8% to 19.4%, moving to active. Others accelerated from 14.9% to 16.4%, shifting from active to strengthening. Fertiliser improved from -4.5% to -0.3%, but remains in decline. The spread narrowed from 32.3 to 20.9 percentage points.
sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Pharma has overtaken Petro Chemicals as the growth leader — likely on export demand and domestic healthcare expansion. Petro Chemicals' sharp deceleration from 27.8% signals cooling downstream demand or capacity saturation. Fertiliser's near-zero contraction suggests stabilisation, but lenders remain cautious.

sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.title: Electronics share rises to 19.9% — Others slip to 80.1%
sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.observation: Within All Engineering, Others (non-Electronics) holds 80.1% share, while Electronics accounts for 19.9%.
sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.direction: Others' share fell from 81.1% to 80.1%, shifting from strengthening to weakening. Electronics rose from 18.9% to 19.9%, moving from weakening to strengthening. The spread narrowed from 62.2 to 60.2 percentage points.
sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.inference: Electronics is gaining share for the first time in this window — a reversal of the prior trend. Lenders are rotating back into consumer electronics, IT hardware, or semiconductor-related capex as traditional engineering growth moderates.

sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.title: Others lead at 34.5% YoY — Electronics strengthens to 17.8%
sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Others (non-Electronics) grew 34.5% year-on-year, while Electronics expanded 17.8%.
sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Others decelerated from 37.5% to 34.5%, remaining active. Electronics accelerated from 13.3% to 17.8%, shifting from active to strengthening. The spread narrowed from 24.2 to 16.7 percentage points.
sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Electronics is re-accelerating after prior moderation — a sign of renewed demand in tech hardware or manufacturing capex. Others remain the growth leader but are slowing, suggesting peak momentum in heavy engineering may have passed.

sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.title: Edible Oils share rises to 11.1% — Sugar slips to 11.1%
sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Food Processing, Others holds 75.9%, Sugar 11.1%, Edible Oils and Vanaspati 11.1%, and Tea 1.9%.
sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.direction: Edible Oils and Vanaspati rose from 10.5% to 11.1%, shifting from strengthening to strengthening. Sugar fell from 11.9% to 11.1%, moving from active to weakening. Others rose from 75.6% to 75.9%, shifting from weakening to active. The spread widened from 73.5 to 74.0 percentage points.
sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.inference: Edible Oils has overtaken Sugar in share for the first time — a structural shift driven by import substitution and domestic capacity expansion. Sugar's share loss signals weakening demand or lender caution on subsidy-dependent economics.

sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.title: Edible Oils surge 33.0% YoY — Sugar and Tea turn negative
sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Edible Oils and Vanaspati grew 33.0% year-on-year, Others 13.6%, Sugar -0.4%, and Tea -3.1%.
sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Edible Oils accelerated from 25.3% to 33.0%, remaining in strengthening. Others decelerated from 14.6% to 13.6%, shifting from strengthening to active. Sugar turned negative from 4.3% to -0.4%, moving from active to declining. Tea contracted further from 0.8% to -3.1%, shifting from strengthening to declining. The spread widened from 24.5 to 36.1 percentage points.
sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Edible Oils is the clear outlier — accelerating to 33.0% on policy support and rising consumption. Sugar and Tea have both turned negative, signalling structural stress in traditional agri-processing segments. Lenders are concentrating exposure in Edible Oils and exiting legacy crops.

sibc-industry-type-share-scan.title: Infrastructure holds 32.8% of industry credit — stable across 19 types
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.observation: Infrastructure accounts for 32.8% of industry credit, Basic Metal and Metal Products 11.3%, and Other Industries 7.5%. Leather and Leather Products holds just 0.3%.
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.direction: Infrastructure's share rose from 32.5% to 32.8%. All 19 industry types remain in active status. The spread widened from 32.2 to 32.5 percentage points.
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.inference: Infrastructure continues to dominate industry credit by a wide margin, reflecting sustained capex in power, roads, and utilities. The stable status across all segments confirms broad-based growth with no major structural shifts — lenders are maintaining diversified exposure across the industrial spectrum.

sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.title: Petroleum surges to 37.8% YoY — 10 of 19 segments strengthening
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.observation: Petroleum, Coal Products and Nuclear Fuels grew 37.8% year-on-year, All Engineering 30.8%, and Gems and Jewellery 26.0%. Leather and Leather Products grew just 6.4%.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.direction: Petroleum accelerated from 32.4% to 37.8%, shifting to strengthening. All Engineering decelerated from 32.2% to 30.8%, remaining active. Gems and Jewellery decelerated sharply from 41.4% to 26.0%, shifting from strengthening to active. Ten of 19 segments are now strengthening, down from twelve. The spread narrowed from 34.2 to 31.3 percentage points. Chemicals and Chemical Products and Paper and Paper Products shifted from active to strengthening; Gems and Jewellery, Basic Metal and Metal Product, and Glass and Glassware shifted from strengthening to active.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.inference: Petroleum has overtaken Gems and Jewellery as the growth leader — likely on energy transition investments and refining capacity expansion. Gems and Jewellery's sharp deceleration from 41.4% to 26.0% signals cooling export demand or working capital normalisation. The shift from twelve to ten strengthening segments suggests growth is concentrating in fewer pockets — watch for divergence between energy/capex-heavy sectors and consumer-facing industries.

sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.title: Power share rises to 56.8% — Telecom weakens within Infrastructure
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Infrastructure, Power holds 56.8%, Roads 22.8%, and Other Infrastructure 12.2%. Railways (other than Indian Railways) accounts for just 0.4%.
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.direction: Power's share rose from 56.5% to 56.8%, remaining active. Roads' share rose from 22.7% to 22.8%, remaining active. Telecommunications shifted from active to weakening. The spread widened from 56.1 to 56.4 percentage points.
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.inference: Power is consolidating its dominance within Infrastructure, reflecting sustained renewable energy and thermal capacity investments. Telecom's shift to weakening confirms structural stress — lenders are reducing exposure as the sector deleverages and capex slows.

sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.title: Ports surge 74.0% YoY — Railways contract -44.1%
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Ports grew 74.0% year-on-year, Power 21.5%, and Other Infrastructure 1.4%. Railways (other than Indian Railways) contracted -44.1%, Airports -26.0%, and Telecommunications -11.9%.
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Ports accelerated from 48.7% to 74.0%, shifting from active to strengthening. Power decelerated from 21.7% to 21.5%, shifting from strengthening to active. Other Infrastructure turned positive from 1.2% to 1.4%, shifting from declining to strengthening. Roads accelerated from 1.2% to an unstated level, shifting from strengthening to active. Railways' contraction improved from -49.8% to -44.1%, remaining in decline. Airports' contraction improved from -27.0% to -26.0%, remaining in decline. Telecom's contraction improved from -12.2% to -11.9%, remaining in decline. The spread widened from 98.5 to 118.1 percentage points.
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Ports is the standout growth driver within Infrastructure — accelerating to 74.0% on trade volume recovery and capacity expansion. Railways, Airports, and Telecom remain in sharp contraction, though all three are moderating — watch for stabilisation signals. The 118.1-point spread is the widest in this domain, confirming extreme divergence between trade/power infrastructure and transport/telecom segments.

sibc-large-corporate-share.title: Large corporates at 67.2% share — lowest in 7 periods
sibc-large-corporate-share.observation: Large corporates account for 67.2% of industry credit in Apr 2026, down 0.08pp from last period.
sibc-large-corporate-share.direction: Share compressed from 71.0% in Jan 2025 to 67.2% in Apr 2026 — a 3.8 percentage point decline over six periods, now at the bottom of the historical range.
sibc-large-corporate-share.inference: Large corporate dominance continues to erode as MSME segments capture incremental lending. The structural shift is accelerating — the largest borrowers are losing ground every period despite their own growth re-acceleration.

sibc-large-corporate-yoy.title: Large corporates hit 10.0% YoY — fastest growth on record
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.observation: Large corporate credit grew 10.0% YoY in Apr 2026, the strongest reading in this seven-period window.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 9.1% last period to 10.0%, extending a recovery that began at 5.5% in Jan 2026 → 7.8% → 9.1% → 10.0%.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.inference: Large corporates are in their strongest growth phase yet, but the 10% pace still trails MSME segments by 10–20 percentage points. They are growing faster than ever in this dataset but still losing market share — the MSME surge is simply too powerful to contain.

sibc-msme-medium-share.title: Medium enterprises hold 9.7% share — highest on record
sibc-msme-medium-share.observation: Medium enterprises account for 9.7% of industry credit in Apr 2026, up 0.07pp from last period.
sibc-msme-medium-share.direction: Share rose from 8.9% in Jan 2025 to 9.7% by Jan 2026 and has held at that peak for four consecutive periods.
sibc-msme-medium-share.inference: Medium enterprises have gained 0.8pp of share over the past year and are now defending that position at the top of the range. The segment is growing fast enough to maintain its foothold but not expanding further — it has plateaued at a new structural level.

sibc-msme-medium-yoy.title: Medium enterprises at 19.9% YoY — growth moderating
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.observation: Medium enterprise credit grew 19.9% YoY in Apr 2026, down 1.96pp from last period.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated from 21.8% last period to 19.9%, the first sub-20% reading since Jan 2026 after peaking at 22.3%.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.inference: Medium enterprises are cooling after three straight periods above 20%, but the 19.9% pace still doubles large corporate growth. The segment remains a strong outperformer — the deceleration is a moderation, not a reversal.

sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.title: Micro & Small grew 32.7% in FY26 vs 8.9% in FY25 — 23.8pp step-up
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.observation: Micro & Small credit grew 8.9% in the year ending Mar 2025 and 32.7% in the year ending Mar 2026, a 23.8 percentage point acceleration.
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.direction: Full-year growth jumped from single digits in FY25 to 30%+ in FY26 — the sharpest year-over-year step-up in this dataset.
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.inference: This is an inflection, not incremental improvement. Micro & Small lending has shifted into a new growth regime, likely driven by policy tailwinds, digital underwriting scale, or supply chain financing expansion. Lenders with exposure here are riding the fastest-growing segment in industry credit.

sibc-msme-micro-small-share.title: Micro & Small at 23.1% share — holding 3pp gain from last year
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.observation: Micro & Small enterprises account for 23.1% of industry credit in Apr 2026, up 0.01pp from last period.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.direction: Share surged from 20.0% in Mar 2025 to 23.5% in Jan 2026, then stabilized at 23.1% — a net gain of 3.1 percentage points in 13 months.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.inference: Micro & Small borrowers have captured 3pp of share from large corporates and are now defending that position. The structural reallocation is complete — banks and NBFCs have aggressively scaled down-market and are holding the new allocation steady.

sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.title: Micro & Small at 30.1% YoY — fourth straight period above 30%
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.observation: Micro & Small credit grew 30.1% YoY in Apr 2026, down 2.59pp from last period but still above 30%.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.direction: Growth exploded from 8.9% in Mar 2025 to 31.2% in Jan 2026, then sustained at 31.2% → 30.4% → 32.7% → 30.1% for four consecutive periods.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.inference: This is the hottest segment in industry credit. Lenders with Micro & Small exposure are seeing 3× the growth of large corporates. The trend is entrenched — four straight periods above 30% signals structural demand, not a one-time spike. The slight deceleration to 30.1% is noise within a sustained boom.

sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.title: MSME outpacing large corporates by 20.1pp — spread narrowing
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.observation: MSME credit is growing 20.1 percentage points faster than large corporate credit in Apr 2026.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.direction: The spread widened from 2.1pp in Mar 2025 to 25.7pp in Jan 2026, then narrowed to 23.5pp → 20.1% as large corporates re-accelerated.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.inference: The growth divergence between MSME and large corporates remains extreme but is narrowing as large corporates hit their fastest pace on record. MSME still dominates incremental credit, but large corporates are clawing back some momentum — the gap is compressing from its Jan 2026 peak.

sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.title: Other Textiles 44.7%, Cotton 36.5% — two segments hold 81% of pool
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Textiles, Other Textiles accounts for 44.7% of credit, Cotton Textiles 36.5%, Man-Made 17.1%, and Jute 1.7%.
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.direction: The four sub-segments span a 42.9 percentage point range, with Other Textiles and Cotton together holding 81% of the pool — unchanged from last period.
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.inference: Textiles credit is concentrated in two legacy categories — Other and Cotton. Man-Made and Jute are marginal. Any shift in Cotton or Other Textiles will move the entire sector.

sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.title: Jute leads Textiles at 19.9% YoY — Other and Cotton shift to active
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.observation: All four Textiles sub-segments are growing, led by Jute at 19.9% YoY, Other Textiles at 13.3%, Cotton at 4.8%, and Man-Made at 2.7%.
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Other Textiles and Man-Made both shifted from strengthening to active status this period, while Jute and Cotton moved to strengthening. The spread widened from 13.9pp to 17.2pp as Jute accelerated and Man-Made slowed.
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Textiles credit remains in broad-based recovery, but momentum is shifting. Jute is pulling away at 19.9%, while the two largest segments (Other and Cotton) have stabilized at mid-teens and low single-digit growth. Man-Made is the laggard at 2.7% — the segment is growing but barely keeping pace with inflation.
